name: mobilenet_v2

members.name: MobileNetV2Config
members.signature: MobileNetV2Config(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, width_mult: float = 1.0, dropout: float = 0.2)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, width_mult: float = 1.0, dropout: float = 0.2)

members.extended:
`width_mult` — uniform channel multiplier; 1.0 = full model.
`dropout`    — classifier dropout probability.

members.name: MobileNetV2

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the ReLU module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

members.summary: MobileNet v2 feature-extracting backbone (no classification head).
members.extended:
Implements the inverted-residual / linear-bottleneck topology
from Sandler et al., "MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear
Bottlenecks", CVPR 2018 ([arXiv:1801.04381](https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.04381)).  Each block expands
a low-dimensional input up by an integer `expand_ratio`, runs
a depthwise $3 \times 3$ convolution in the wider interior
space, then projects back to a narrow bottleneck — *without* a
final non-linearity (the "linear bottleneck").  When stride and
channel counts match, an identity shortcut is added around the
whole block:

$$
y = x + \mathrm{Proj}\big(
    \mathrm{DW}\big( \mathrm{Expand}(x) \big)
\big).
$$

The body is a 3×3 stem (stride 2) followed by seven inverted-residual
stages and a final 1×1 head expansion to 1280 channels.  A global
average pool collapses the final feature map to a single 1×1
descriptor.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use the factory functions
(`mobilenet_v2`, `mobilenet_v2_075`) for
paper-cited width-multiplier variants.

examples:
Build a MobileNet-v2 backbone and run a forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet_v2 import mobilenet_v2
>>> backbone = mobilenet_v2()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(2, 1280, 1, 1)
notes:
The inverted-residual block is the *inverse* of the classical
bottleneck shape: instead of wide → narrow → wide, it does
narrow → wide → narrow, with the residual connection on the
*narrow* (low-dimensional) tensor.  Combined with the linear
bottleneck (no ReLU on the projection), this preserves the
representational capacity of the narrow channels while letting
the wide interior use ReLU6 freely.  The result is roughly
$30\%$ fewer parameters and FLOPs than MobileNet-v1 at
comparable accuracy.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: features
attributes.description:
Stem + seven inverted-residual stages + 1×1 head expansion.
Channel counts scale with `config.width_mult`.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Global average pool collapsing the final feature map to
`(B, C, 1, 1)`.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Per-stage descriptor (channels + reduction factor) exposed
via `BackboneMixin` for downstream FPN / decoder
modules.

members.name: MobileNetV2ForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: MobileNet v2 with global-average-pooled linear classification head.
members.extended:
Combines a `MobileNetV2` backbone with the standard
ImageNet classification head: an `lucid.nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d`
to pool every spatial location into a single feature vector,
a `lucid.nn.Dropout` (probability `config.dropout`,
default 0.2), and a `lucid.nn.Linear` projection to
`config.num_classes` logits.  When `labels` are supplied to
`forward`, a cross-entropy loss is computed and returned
alongside the logits.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use the `*_cls` factory functions
(`mobilenet_v2_cls`, `mobilenet_v2_075_cls`) for
paper-cited configurations.

examples:
Run inference on a batch of 224×224 RGB images:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet_v2 import mobilenet_v2_cls
>>> model = mobilenet_v2_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(4, 1000)
Retarget to CIFAR-10:
>>> model = mobilenet_v2_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> model.config.num_classes
10
notes:
The classification flow is



$$
\text{logits} = W \,\operatorname{Drop}\!\left(
    \operatorname{GAP}(\,\mathrm{backbone}(x)\,)
\right) + b,
$$

where $\operatorname{GAP}$ is the global average pool.
Loss is the standard categorical cross-entropy, computed only
when `labels` is not `None`.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Global average pool collapsing the final feature map to
`1 × 1`.

attributes.name: drop
attributes.description:
Dropout layer (probability `config.dropout`) applied
before the linear classifier.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Linear projection from the 1280-ch head (scaled by
`max(1.0, width_mult)`) to `config.num_classes`.

members.name: mobilenet_v2
members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV2` with the canonical paper topology
from Sandler et al., 2018 (Table 2): seven inverted-residual
stages with block repeats `(1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 3, 1)` and channel
progression `16 → 24 → 32 → 64 → 96 → 160 → 320`, followed
by a 1×1 head expansion to 1280 channels.  Approximately
3.5M parameters and 72.0% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy (Table 4).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored — the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `MobileNetV2Config`
(e.g. `in_channels=1` for grayscale input).

returns.description:
Backbone with the MobileNet-v2 ($\alpha = 1.0$)
configuration applied (or with `overrides` merged on top
of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet_v2 import mobilenet_v2
>>> model = mobilenet_v2()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 1280, 1, 1)
notes:
See Sandler et al., "MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear
Bottlenecks", CVPR 2018 ([arXiv:1801.04381](https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.04381)), Table 2 and Table 4.

members.name: mobilenet_v2_cls
members.signature: mobilenet_v2_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: MobileNetV2Weights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: MobileNet-v2 image classifier at width multiplier $\alpha = 1.0$.
members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV2ForImageClassification` with the
canonical paper topology (Sandler et al., 2018) followed by
global average pooling and a linear projection to
`config.num_classes` (default 1000 for ImageNet-1k).
Approximately 3.5M parameters and 72.0% ImageNet-1k top-1
accuracy.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag
(`MobileNetV2Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1`); a tag string (e.g.
`"IMAGENET1K_V1"`) → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually
exclusive with `weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member, e.g.
`MobileNetV2Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1`.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `MobileNetV2Config`
(typically `num_classes` to retarget the classifier).
Note: overriding `num_classes` away from the checkpoint's
class count makes pretrained loading fail the strict key/shape
check — load with a matching head, then call
`reset_classifier`.

returns.description:
Classifier with the MobileNet-v2 ($\alpha = 1.0$)
configuration applied (or with `overrides` merged on top
of it), optionally initialised from pretrained weights.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet_v2 import mobilenet_v2_cls
>>> model = mobilenet_v2_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 10)
Load ImageNet-pretrained weights:
>>> model = mobilenet_v2_cls(pretrained=True)            # DEFAULT tag
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet_v2 import MobileNetV2Weights
>>> model = mobilenet_v2_cls(weights=MobileNetV2Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1)
notes:
See Sandler et al., "MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear
Bottlenecks", CVPR 2018 ([arXiv:1801.04381](https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.04381)), Table 4.  Pretrained
weights are converted from torchvision's `MobileNet_V2_Weights`
and hosted on the Hugging Face Hub under `lucid-dl/mobilenet-v2`.

members.name: mobilenet_v2_075
members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV2` with every channel count multiplied
by 0.75 — approximately 2.6M parameters.  Sandler et al., 2018
(Table 4) report 69.8% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy with this
configuration, at roughly 60% of the FLOPs of the full-width
model.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

returns.description:
Backbone with the MobileNet-v2 ($\alpha = 0.75$)
configuration applied (or with `overrides` merged on top
of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet_v2 import mobilenet_v2_075
>>> model = mobilenet_v2_075()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 1280, 1, 1)
notes:
See Sandler et al., "MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear
Bottlenecks", CVPR 2018 ([arXiv:1801.04381](https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.04381)), Table 4.  The head
expansion remains at 1280 channels — narrow variants do *not*
shrink the head below 1280.

members.name: mobilenet_v2_075_cls
members.summary: MobileNet-v2 image classifier at width multiplier $\alpha = 0.75$.
members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV2ForImageClassification` with the
paper-cited 0.75-width topology — approximately 2.6M parameters
and 69.8% ImageNet-1k top-1 in Sandler et al., 2018 (Table 4).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

returns.description:
Classifier with the MobileNet-v2 ($\alpha = 0.75$)
configuration applied (or with `overrides` merged on top
of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet_v2 import mobilenet_v2_075_cls
>>> model = mobilenet_v2_075_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
See Sandler et al., "MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear
Bottlenecks", CVPR 2018 ([arXiv:1801.04381](https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.04381)), Table 4.

members.name: MobileNetV2Weights
members.extended:
Ships a single ImageNet-1k checkpoint (`IMAGENET1K_V1`) —
the V1 weights distributed by the reference-framework model zoo,
re-hosted under `huggingface.co/lucid-dl/mobilenet-v2` with the
official `acc@1=71.878 / acc@5=90.286` validation metrics.

summary: MobileNet v2 family — Sandler et al., 2018.
citation: Sandler, Mark, et al. "MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear Bottlenecks." Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2018, pp. 4510–4520.
theory:
MobileNet-v2 replaces the plain depthwise-separable stack of
v1 with the **inverted residual bottleneck** block.  Each block
receives a low-dimensional tensor of $c$ channels, projects
it *up* to a much wider $t \cdot c$ channel space with a
$1 \times 1$ convolution, applies a $3 \times 3$
depthwise convolution in that expanded space, then projects
back *down* to $c'$ channels with another $1 \times 1$.
When the input and output shapes match, an identity shortcut is
added around the whole block:



$$
y = x + \mathrm{Proj}\big(\mathrm{DW}\big(\mathrm{Expand}(x)\big)\big).
$$

This is the *inverse* of the classical bottleneck shape (which is
wide → narrow → wide), and is why the authors call it an
"inverted residual".

The second insight is the **linear bottleneck**.  The final
$1 \times 1$ projection is followed by *no* non-linearity —
a deliberate departure from the ReLU-after-every-conv pattern.
The paper's manifold argument shows that ReLU on a low-dimensional
activation collapses information that cannot be recovered;
keeping the bottleneck output linear preserves the representational
capacity of the narrow channels while still letting the wide
interior of the block use ReLU6 freely.

These two ideas together give a network that, at comparable
accuracy to MobileNet-v1, uses roughly $30\%$ fewer
parameters and $30\%$ fewer multiply-adds, and runs
measurably faster on mobile CPUs.  As with v1, a single
**width multiplier** $\alpha$ scales every channel count
uniformly, exposing the same accuracy/latency trade-off curve
without retraining.
